content.text: Automated Classification Rules for AI Data

Manual data classification does not scale to Gen AI workloads. A busy enterprise deployment may handle thousands of prompts per hour, each requiring a sensitivity decision before the model call proceeds. Automated classification systems replace manual tagging with deterministic rules and machine-learning models that run in the ingestion path, producing a label, a confidence score, and an audit trail in milliseconds.

This topic covers how to build and operate an automated classification system: the three-stage pipeline (structural rules → pattern library → ML classifier), rule management, confidence gating, feedback loops, and the metrics that determine whether the classifier is reliable enough to trust with enforcement decisions.

A single-stage classifier is brittle. Pure regex fails on novel phrasings; pure ML has unpredictable error modes; pure human review is too slow. A three-stage pipeline uses each technique where it is most reliable:
Deterministic; zero false negatives on exact formats (SSNs, card numbers, API keys)
Can't interpret context; false positives on similar formats
Handles semantic variants; catches codenames, near-matches
Requires maintained entity lists; slower than regex
Handles context; generalises to unseen content; produces confidence score
Probabilistic; requires training data; drifts over time

A result from Stage 1 is emitted immediately without proceeding to later stages. If Stage 1 produces no high-confidence match, Stage 2 runs; if Stage 2 is also inconclusive, Stage 3 runs. This keeps median latency at Stage 1 levels while reserving the slower stages for genuinely ambiguous content.

Structural rules detect content whose sensitivity can be determined from its format alone — credit card numbers, social security numbers, API keys, IP addresses. These patterns are exact and deterministic: a valid Luhn-checked card number in a prompt is REGULATED PCI regardless of surrounding context.

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import IntEnum

class SensitivityLevel(IntEnum):
    PUBLIC = 0; INTERNAL = 1; CONFIDENTIAL = 2; RESTRICTED = 3; REGULATED = 4
    def label(self) -> str: return self.name

@dataclass
class ClassificationRule:
    rule_id: str
    name: str
    pattern: re.Pattern
    label: SensitivityLevel
    category: str
    enabled: bool = True

    def match(self, text: str) -> list[str]:
        return self.pattern.findall(text)


def _luhn(number: str) -> bool:
    digits = [int(d) for d in number if d.isdigit()]
    odd = digits[-1::-2]
    even = [d * 2 - 9 if d * 2 > 9 else d * 2 for d in digits[-2::-2]]
    return (sum(odd) + sum(even)) % 10 == 0


STRUCTURAL_RULES: list[ClassificationRule] = [
    ClassificationRule(
        "SR-001", "SSN",
        re.compile(r"\b(?!000|666|9\d{2})\d{3}-(?!00)\d{2}-(?!0000)\d{4}\b"),
        SensitivityLevel.REGULATED, "PII",
    ),
    ClassificationRule(
        "SR-002", "Visa/Mastercard/Amex",
        re.compile(r"\b(?:4[0-9]{12}(?:[0-9]{3})?|5[1-5][0-9]{14}|3[47][0-9]{13})\b"),
        SensitivityLevel.REGULATED, "PCI",
    ),
    ClassificationRule(
        "SR-003", "OpenAI API key",
        re.compile(r"\bsk-[A-Za-z0-9]{32,}\b"),
        SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED, "CREDENTIALS",
    ),
    ClassificationRule(
        "SR-004", "GitHub PAT",
        re.compile(r"\bghp_[A-Za-z0-9]{36}\b"),
        SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED, "CREDENTIALS",
    ),
    ClassificationRule(
        "SR-005", "IPv4 address (private ranges)",
        re.compile(r"\b(?:10|172\.(?:1[6-9]|2\d|3[01])|192\.168)\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}\b"),
        SensitivityLevel.CONFIDENTIAL, "IP",
    ),
    ClassificationRule(
        "SR-006", "Email address",
        re.compile(r"\b[a-zA-Z0-9._%+-]+@[a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.[a-z]{2,}\b"),
        SensitivityLevel.CONFIDENTIAL, "PII",
    ),
    ClassificationRule(
        "SR-007", "High-entropy string (likely secret)",
        re.compile(r"[A-Za-z0-9+/]{40,}={0,2}"),
        SensitivityLevel.RESTRICTED, "CREDENTIALS",
    ),
]


@dataclass
class StageResult:
    matched_rules: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    label: SensitivityLevel = SensitivityLevel.INTERNAL
    categories: set[str] = field(default_factory=set)
    confident: bool = False   # True if any rule fired

def run_structural_rules(text: str,
                         rules: list[ClassificationRule] = STRUCTURAL_RULES
                         ) -> StageResult:
    result = StageResult()
    for rule in rules:
        if not rule.enabled:
            continue
        matches = rule.match(text)
        if not matches:
            continue
        # PCI: validate card with Luhn
        if rule.category == "PCI":
            matches = [m for m in matches if _luhn(re.sub(r"\D", "", m))]
        if matches:
            result.matched_rules.append(rule.rule_id)
            result.categories.add(rule.category)
            if rule.label > result.label:
                result.label = rule.label
            result.confident = True
    return result

Stage 2 handles content whose sensitivity depends on vocabulary rather than format — clinical terminology, legal privilege markers, financial non-public information. A named-entity recognition (NER) model identifies entities in context; a keyword dictionary catches domain-specific terms the NER model may miss.

The ML classifier handles content that passes through Stages 1 and 2 without a confident match — content whose sensitivity is implied by context rather than explicit markers. A fine-tuned text classification model produces a probability distribution over the five sensitivity levels and a set of category probabilities.

The aggregator combines results from whichever stages ran, applies the maximum-label rule, and produces a single ClassificationResult. The confidence gate decides whether the result is reliable enough to use for enforcement, or whether it should be sent to human review.

import uuid
from datetime import datetime
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class ClassificationResult:
    trace_id: str
    text_length: int
    label: SensitivityLevel
    categories: set[str]
    confidence: float              # 0.0–1.0; 1.0 for pure structural matches
    matched_rule_ids: list[str]
    needs_human_review: bool
    classified_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)

    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "trace_id": self.trace_id,
            "label": self.label.label(),
            "categories": sorted(self.categories),
            "confidence": round(self.confidence, 4),
            "matched_rule_ids": self.matched_rule_ids,
            "needs_human_review": self.needs_human_review,
            "classified_at": self.classified_at.isoformat(),
        }


CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD = 0.85

def classify(text: str,
             ml_classifier: SensitivityClassifier | None = None
             ) -> ClassificationResult:
    trace_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    all_rules: list[str] = []
    label = SensitivityLevel.INTERNAL
    categories: set[str] = set()
    confidence = 1.0

    # Stage 1: structural rules (deterministic → confidence = 1.0)
    s1 = run_structural_rules(text)
    all_rules.extend(s1.matched_rules)
    categories.update(s1.categories)
    if s1.label > label:
        label = s1.label

    if s1.confident:
        # High-certainty structural match — skip later stages
        return ClassificationResult(
            trace_id, len(text), label, categories, 1.0,
            all_rules, needs_human_review=False
        )

    # Stage 2: pattern library
    s2 = run_pattern_library(text)
    all_rules.extend(s2.matched_rules)
    categories.update(s2.categories)
    if s2.label > label:
        label = s2.label

    if s2.confident:
        return ClassificationResult(
            trace_id, len(text), label, categories, 0.95,
            all_rules, needs_human_review=False
        )

    # Stage 3: ML classifier (if available)
    if ml_classifier is not None:
        prediction = ml_classifier.predict(text)
        s3 = ml_classifier.to_stage_result(prediction)
        all_rules.extend(s3.matched_rules)
        categories.update(prediction.categories)
        if prediction.label > label:
            label = prediction.label
        confidence = prediction.label_confidence
    else:
        confidence = 0.5   # no ML model configured

    needs_review = confidence < CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD
    return ClassificationResult(
        trace_id, len(text), label, categories, confidence,
        all_rules, needs_human_review=needs_review
    )


# Example
result = classify("Draft a severance agreement for Sarah Chen, SSN 123-45-6789")
print(result.to_dict())
# label: REGULATED, categories: [PII], confidence: 1.0, needs_human_review: False

Classification rules are code: they must be versioned, tested, and deployed through a review process. An unreviewed rule change can cause silent mis-classification that only surfaces when a compliance audit queries the audit log.
New rule written with pattern, test cases, and expected label
Run against labelled test corpus; measure precision/recall
Second DG engineer reviews pattern logic and edge cases
No false negatives on REGULATED category
Deploy to shadow mode: classify but don't enforce; compare with production
Mark disabled; retain in registry for audit trail
Confirm no active pipelines depend on rule_id

Every rule — structural or keyword — must be recorded in a version-controlled registry (a JSON file in git) with its rule_id, version, category, label, author, test_precision, test_recall, and status. The registry is the single source of truth for what is deployed in production and is queried by the audit log to explain why a specific label was assigned to a given trace_id.

A classifier that is 95% accurate sounds impressive but means 1 in 20 prompts is mis-classified. For REGULATED content, a false negative (missing the label) is a compliance failure; a false positive (over-labelling) restricts access unnecessarily. Track both separately and set different thresholds for each direction.
Precision, Recall, and F1 per Label
False negatives (missing PUBLIC → over-restricting) acceptable; false positives (labelling sensitive as PUBLIC) critical
Business risk if confidential data under-classified
High risk; few false negatives tolerated
Regulatory risk; near-zero false negatives required

Human review decisions — where the confidence gate escalated to a curator — are the highest-quality training signal available. Every human-confirmed label should be fed back into the training pipeline to improve the ML classifier over time. Without a feedback loop, the classifier drifts as new content types appear in prompts.
Multiple rules may fire on the same text and disagree on the label or category. The conflict resolution strategy must be explicit and auditable.
Stage 1 says REGULATED; Stage 3 says CONFIDENTIAL
Pattern matches both PII and CREDENTIALS in same text
Assign both categories; label = max(PII_label, CREDENTIALS_label)
Rule SR-003 disabled; ML detects credentials anyway
ML result stands; log that SR-003 was disabled
User claims INTERNAL; classifier returns CONFIDENTIAL
Classifier wins; log the override for audit
User claims REGULATED; classifier returns CONFIDENTIAL
User declaration wins (conservative); flag for periodic audit to detect gaming

Before enabling enforcement for a new rule or a retrained ML model, run it in shadow mode for at least two weeks. In shadow mode, the classifier produces a result but does not enforce it — the decision goes to the existing (older) classifier for enforcement. Shadow results are logged for comparison.

def run_shadow_comparison(text: str,
                          production_classify,
                          shadow_classify,
                          audit_log) -> ClassificationResult:
    prod = production_classify(text)
    shadow = shadow_classify(text)
    audit_log._append({
        "event": "shadow_comparison",
        "trace_id": prod.trace_id,
        "production_label": prod.label.label(),
        "shadow_label": shadow.label.label(),
        "agreed": prod.label == shadow.label,
    })
    return prod   # only production result is enforced
Before enabling classification enforcement in a production Gen AI pipeline, verify each of the following:
Structural rules have unit tests covering valid + invalid formats
Classifier precision/recall above thresholds for all five labels
Shadow mode agreement rate ≥98% for 2 weeks before promotion
Rule registry version-controlled; changes require PR review

Automated classification for Gen AI data requires a layered system that combines deterministic structural rules with probabilistic ML-based classification, confidence gating, and human review for uncertain cases. Key design principles:
(structural → pattern library → ML) runs fast layers first, reserving slower stages for ambiguous content.
ensures that if any stage fires at a higher level, the higher label wins — never downgrade a label at the aggregation step.
routes low-confidence ML predictions to human review rather than silently mis-classifying.
prevent unreviewed rule changes from causing silent mis-classification in production.
— REGULATED requires 99% precision and 98% recall; lower labels have relaxed thresholds — reflect the different risks of over- and under-classification.
feeds human review decisions back to the ML training pipeline, preventing classifier drift as new content types appear.
defaults to the maximum label and documents the resolution in the audit trail.

A well-tuned automated classification system makes sensitivity enforcement a background operation that does not slow down Gen AI workflows — the median prompt takes fewer than 5ms to classify, and the audit trail proves it at every step.
